title: Guest Designer Laura Umansky on Systems, Scaling, and Studio Operations
summary: Erin Stetzer hosted Houston and Aspen-based interior designer Laura Umansky for an open Q&A session covering her 18-year firm's evolution, business systems, team retention, billing structures, and client management strategies. Laura shared candid insights on scaling a design firm, establishing project minimums, and why hourly billing is the only model she will use. Participants asked questions ranging from procurement software to designer licensing to managing client add-on requests.

teachings.concept: Hourly Billing as the Only Viable Fee Structure
teachings.explanation: Laura argues that hourly billing is the only model that allows a design firm to remain flexible and say yes to scope changes without conflict. Fixed-fee and percentage-of-construction models create friction when projects evolve, whereas hourly billing keeps the client in the driver's seat and eliminates scope disputes.
teachings.quote: I am a big believer that hourly billing is the only way to operate and I will die on that hill.

teachings.concept: Establishing Project Minimums Based on Data
teachings.explanation: Rather than taking any project that comes in, Laura's firm uses data from post-mortems on past projects to identify the minimum investment level at which their team does their best work and clients are happiest. Minimums are not arbitrary; they are derived from analyzing which projects produced the best outcomes for both clients and team.
teachings.quote: We just took a really hard look at what those were and just said, okay, if we're working under that line, it's gray... it's really just data driven.

teachings.explanation: Laura's firm tracks how many billable hours each role is expected to produce weekly, which allows her to understand profitability by role. Senior staff have lower utilization due to management responsibilities, while junior staff are highly utilized on production work. This data drives hiring and fee decisions.
teachings.quote: A design director is probably only billing about 20 hours a week for a project... their utilization rate is 50%... a junior designer gets paid less, but they're billing like almost 85% utilized.

teachings.concept: Ad Projects as a Structured Expansion Model
teachings.explanation: When a client wants to continue work after a project completes, Laura's firm treats it as a completely separate 'ad project' with its own internal tracking, invoicing, and project name. This keeps financials and timelines clean and prevents scope from bleeding across engagements.
teachings.quote: We create a separate project. We don't necessarily do a new contract, but we treat it as a completely separate project and we don't muddy it with the existing project.

teachings.explanation: When a client wants to start a new phase but the team is not ready, Laura's firm begins work slowly and deliberately to maintain client engagement without overextending resources. They are transparent with clients about the pacing and set a realistic kickoff timeline.
teachings.quote: We call it like bread crumbing it out where we start really slowly, but we keep them engaged... we're also transparent with them about that.

teachings.explanation: Laura believes employees are motivated by different things including money, recognition, and knowledge growth. Effective retention requires understanding what drives each individual and tailoring incentives accordingly. Universal perks like hybrid work and open PTO provide a foundation, while personalized approaches address individual motivators.
teachings.quote: I figure out what motivates someone and everyone is motivated by different things... Are they money driven? Are they recognition driven? Are they knowledge driven?

teachings.concept: Proactive Hiring Based on Pipeline and Utilization
teachings.explanation: Laura hires proactively by monitoring project pipeline and team utilization rather than waiting until the team is overwhelmed. Because senior hires can take six months to recruit, she begins the process well before the need becomes critical.
teachings.quote: When I see that we're four months down the line and still fully utilized, then that's my pain point... it's really hard to recruit a senior level person on the fly. Like that can take six months easily.

teachings.concept: Focusing Exclusively on Your Sweet Spot
teachings.explanation: Laura's attempt to serve the mid-market through a lower-price-point offshoot called Studio You failed because the same level of effort was required for less revenue, and that market segment demands more attention per dollar spent. The lesson is that trying to serve multiple market segments simultaneously dilutes profitability and team satisfaction.
teachings.quote: You're still paying the same amount for a designer on your team to design a middle of the road project. And it takes just as much work, if not more, because that budget and that investment means so much more to that mid-level client.

actionable_advice: Move project management and team communication into a dedicated platform like Basecamp rather than relying on spreadsheets for shared information.
Use Studio Designer or a similar purpose-built tool for furnishings procurement rather than managing it in spreadsheets alone.
Give clients a fee range estimate upfront even when billing hourly, broken out by phase such as construction design and interior design, so they have a predictable expectation.
Track every employee's billable hours weekly against a target utilization rate so you always know the profitability of each role on your team.
Calculate the full cost of each employee including benefits and overhead, then model how many billable hours they need to produce to cover that cost and generate profit.
When a client wants to continue into a new phase of work, create a separate project with its own name, internal tracking, and invoicing rather than blending it with the original project.
When your team cannot immediately start an add-on project, breadcrumb the work slowly while being transparent with the client about the timeline for a full kickoff.
Conduct post-mortems on completed projects to identify which types of projects produced the best outcomes for clients and team, then use that data to set your minimums.
Hold a structured project kickoff with all stakeholders including the builder, designer, and client to align on decision deadlines and keep design selections on schedule.
Set clear brand representation expectations with your team so they know how to conduct themselves when networking or attending industry events on the company's behalf.
client_challenges: A design-build participant was overwhelmed by the volume of systems and templates marketed on social media and struggling to identify a holistic solution for FF&E and project management.
A design-build owner was unsure how to handle clients who want to immediately continue into add-on work when the team is already booked out on subsequent projects.
A builder asked how to encourage designers to deliver selections decisions on time when designer delays threaten the project timeline.
A design-build participant considering adding furnishings services asked how interior design firms structure their fees and what compensation ranges look like for strong designers.
A participant asked how to know when it is the right time to hire and what signals indicate that growth is necessary.
A participant asked how to retain top employees over the long term, especially when those employees could leave for other firms or start their own businesses.
A participant building her portfolio asked how much weight prospective clients and employers place on professional licensing versus experience and body of work.
notable_quotes: On hourly billing: 'I am a big believer that hourly billing is the only way to operate and I will die on that hill.'
On setting minimums: 'It's really just data driven. We just took a really hard look at what those were and said, if we're working under that line, our projects can be successful.'
On licensing: 'Zero. I've never been licensed. It's never been an ambition of mine... I don't think it's an indication of anything.'

On the mid-market experiment: 'It takes just as much work, if not more, because that budget and that investment means so much more to that mid-level client or homeowner. Every penny has to go really far.'
On sharing knowledge openly: 'We both think all ships rise with the tide... I feel like we think we're right, and so we want to share that. Until we're wrong.'

On promoting employees who later leave: 'We've promoted other designers pretty heavily that have gone on to start their own firms... as a business owner you're like, damn, I invested a lot of money in that. But I think there's not really much you can control. It's not personal ever either.'
